items.title: US CPI April 3.8% — highest in 3 years, well above Fed's 3% band
items.idea: US headline CPI hit 3.8% in April, continuing an upward trend and sitting 80bps above the Fed's 3% upper band — highest in three years. Energy (Hormuz blockage) is the accelerant, but core ex-energy at 2.8%, sticky prices back above 3%, and Supercore (services ex-shelter) rising sharply. The underlying trend was already turning before oil spiked.
items.angle: Immediate market read: Warsh arrives as incoming Fed chair into a stagflation setup, not a soft landing. The 'transitory energy' defense only works if Hormuz opens — and Trump just called the peace proposal garbage.
items.tweetA: US CPI April: 3.8%. Fed upper band: 3%. Gap: 80bps and widening. Sticky prices above 3%. Supercore rising sharply. The Hormuz blockage is the accelerant, not the cause. The underlying inflation trend was already turning before oil spiked.
items.tweetB: 3.8% CPI is the number everyone's watching. The number nobody's talking about: even ex-energy, inflation is still rising across median, trimmed mean, and sticky measures. Strip out oil and you still have a problem. The Fed can't fix Hormuz — but they also can't ignore what's underneath it.

charts.desc: Three charts: (1) headline CPI 3-year trend showing current 3.8% reacceleration, (2) CPI ex-energy showing contained but rising 2.8% trend, (3) multiple core measures (sticky, trimmed mean, supercore) all turning up simultaneously — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Real wages turned negative — CPI outpaced hourly earnings for first time since 2022
items.idea: In April, consumer prices rose faster than average hourly earnings for the first time since 2022. Atlanta Fed wage tracker: +3.9% in March vs CPI +3.8% — the gap is essentially zero and closing. This never happened during Trump's first term.
items.angle: Political risk meter: when wages go negative in real terms and stay there, consumer confidence collapses. This is the variable that changes legislative math faster than any market move.
items.tweetA: Consumer prices rose faster than wages in April — first time since 2022. The gap between Atlanta Fed wages (+3.9%) and CPI (+3.8%) is basically zero. At current trajectory it goes negative. That didn't happen in Trump 1.0. It's happening in Trump 2.0.
items.tweetB: The inflation number that actually moves voters isn't CPI. It's whether their paycheck is keeping up. It's not. The affordability squeeze is back. Watch consumer confidence and retail sales in the next 6 weeks — those will tell you the political consequences faster than any poll.

charts.desc: Chart showing wage growth vs CPI trend — convergence to near-zero real wage growth.

items.title: Kevin Warsh confirmed Fed governor — 2yr yields at 4%, market prices next move as HIKE
items.idea: Kevin Warsh confirmed as Fed governor today (replacing ultra-Trumpy Stephen Miran), with chair confirmation expected. He arrives as 2-year Treasury yields touch 4% — highest since June last year — and the market is pricing the next Fed move as a hike rather than a cut.
items.angle: The political setup is brutal: Trump wants cuts, Warsh inherits a stagflation setup. His first task is credibly framing why rates stay put. If he fails to anchor expectations, the front end moves fast.
items.tweetA: Kevin Warsh confirmed at the Fed today. He inherits: 2yr yields at 4%, hike now more likely than cut, inflation at 3.8%, and an energy shock with no clear resolution. That's not the brief any incoming chair wants. How he frames expectations in his first public comments is THE trade.
items.tweetB: Warsh replaces Miran — the most Trumpian Fed pick — just as the market prices in hikes. The irony: Trump pushed for aggressive accommodation and got a Fed that now can't cut. The Iran war broke his rate strategy. Watch the White House vs. Fed tension build.

charts.desc: 2yr yield chart touching 4%; Fed funds rate expectations chart showing hike probability surpassing cut probability.

items.title: Trump calls Iran peace proposal 'garbage' — December Brent retouches $90
items.idea: Trump dismissed Iran's peace proposal as 'garbage,' sending December Brent crude futures back above $90/barrel. This is the forward curve, not spot — someone is paying $90 for oil delivery in six months. The Strait of Hormuz blockage is pricing in as a structural feature.
items.angle: $90 December Brent is a recession signal for every energy-importing economy. DAX, Nikkei, and CSI 300 are all levered to cheap energy access. If this persists into Q3, global equity re-ratings follow.
items.tweetA: Trump: Iran peace proposal is 'garbage.' Brent Dec futures: back above $90. That's not spot panic — that's the forward curve pricing a structural Hormuz premium into year-end. Someone is paying $90 for oil six months from now. Every energy-importing economy is being stress-tested.
items.tweetB: The market keeps hoping for an Iran resolution and keeps getting disappointed. Short vol on Iran headlines is the wrong trade. Trump's 'garbage' comment is a signal: the administration has no urgency on this. $90 Brent is the new floor until proven otherwise. $VLO $MPC $XOM

charts.desc: Brent crude futures December retouching $90 — forward curve not just spot. CHART WORTH SAVING.

items.title: Jensen Huang boards Air Force One last-minute for Trump's Beijing summit
items.idea: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang was added last-minute to Trump's China delegation, boarding Air Force One on a stopover in Alaska. Full delegation: Musk, Tim Cook (Apple), Kelly Ortberg (Boeing), David Solomon (Goldman Sachs). Trump's first ask to Xi: 'open up' China. First Beijing visit in nearly a decade.
items.angle: Jensen's presence signals H200 chip sales are on the table. Policy framework: H200 licensed (older chips open), Blackwell restricted. Jensen is there to close H200 deal and protect Blackwell/Vera Rubin line.
items.tweetA: Jensen Huang just boarded Air Force One to Beijing — last minute addition alongside Musk, Tim Cook, Goldman's Solomon, Boeing's Ortberg. Trump's first ask to Xi: 'open up' China to US business. This isn't trade diplomacy. It's a $NVDA sales trip with Air Force One as the backdrop.
items.tweetB: The 'will he won't he get invited' drama around Jensen and China tells you everything about the internal politics of AI export controls. The administration is threading: H200s open, Blackwell closed. Jensen lobbied for exactly this structure. Now he's on the plane to close it.

items.title: H200s licensed for China but NONE exported — China won't let its companies buy
items.idea: Commerce Secretary Lutnick confirmed H200 chips are licensed for sale to China but none have been exported because the Chinese government hasn't allowed its tech companies to purchase them. US gets a 25% fee from Nvidia on any H200s sold to China. Huang maintains support for restrictions on Blackwell and Vera Rubin.
items.angle: The deal exists on paper. Jensen is on a plane to close it. A 25% government take on chip exports is a new precedent for how Washington monetizes tech export policy — changes the incentive structure entirely.
items.tweetA: Buried in the H200 story: Commerce confirmed that H200s are LICENSED for sale to China but ZERO have been exported — because China hasn't allowed its tech companies to buy them. The deal exists. It hasn't happened. Jensen is on a plane to Beijing to change that.
items.tweetB: The US government gets a 25% fee on every H200 Nvidia sells to China. Revenue-split on AI chip exports. If this closes, it's a new model for how Washington monetizes tech export controls. The incentive to let the deal happen is now built into federal revenue. Think about what that means for future 'restrictions.'

items.title: Bipartisan AI Overwatch Act: block Blackwell sales, congressional oversight of H200 licenses
items.idea: House lawmakers advanced bipartisan export control bills including the AI Overwatch Act — would block Blackwell chip sales to China AND give Congress oversight of H200 license applications. Direct countermove to whatever Trump promises Xi. Legislative vs executive on AI export policy.
items.angle: Bipartisan agreement on China tech controls survives administrations. If AI Overwatch Act passes, Jensen's Air Force One diplomacy gets legally constrained.
items.tweetA: Congress is moving the AI Overwatch Act — bipartisan bill to block Blackwell chip sales to China AND give Congress oversight of H200 license applications. This is the legislative counterpunch to whatever Trump promises Xi in Beijing. Executive deal vs. congressional constraint. Watch which wins. $NVDA
items.tweetB: Rare bipartisan consensus in Congress: restrict AI chip exports to China more aggressively. The AI Overwatch Act specifically targets Blackwell — the chip Jensen is trying to protect. If this passes, the architecture of US AI export policy shifts from executive discretion to congressional approval. Structurally different risk.

items.title: AI capex = single largest cross-border capital flow of 2024-2026, driving US equity premium
items.idea: AI capex is the single largest cross-border capital flow of 2024–2026. Foreigners bidding US mega-cap tech because the US is the center of AI infrastructure buildout. This explains why US valuations are at all-time highs while every other major equity index has underperformed. Traditional central bank balance sheet models are missing this driver entirely.
items.angle: Every quant model without AI capex cross-border flow input is working incomplete. US tech premium persists as long as AI buildout stays US-centric. Risk: if buildout diversifies globally, premium degrades.
items.tweetA: AI capex is the single largest cross-border capital flow of 2024–2026. Foreigners bidding US mega cap tech because that's where the infrastructure build is. That's why US valuations are at all-time highs while every other major index underperforms. It's not multiple expansion — it's flow mechanics. And most macro models are missing it.
items.tweetB: Every traditional liquidity model is working with incomplete inputs right now. Central bank balance sheets tell one story. AI capex cross-border flows tell another. The second story is bigger. When the buildout stays US-centric, US equity premium holds. Watch what happens if it doesn't.

charts.desc: Slide deck mentioned — Charts inside, worth opening

items.title: First Hyperliquid ETF (THYP) launched — bullish for PURR not bearish
items.idea: First Hyperliquid ETF (THYP) launched today. Contrarian read: bullish for PURR. Regulators allowing the ETF signals regulatory pathway clearing. But an ETF structurally cannot do share buybacks, run a credit facility, or bridge CeDeFi networks. Every other crypto treasury company diluted shareholders; PURR actively bought back shares at discount.
items.angle: ETF vs. active treasury is the SPY vs. Berkshire debate. ETF sets the floor and benchmark. PURR needs to demonstrate active value creation above spot hype — credit facility and TradeXYZ validator partnership are the differentiators.
items.tweetA: First Hyperliquid ETF (THYP) launched today. Hot take: this is BULLISH for PURR. Regulators clearing the ETF means the path forward is open. But an ETF can't buy back its own shares when it trades at a discount. Can't run a credit facility. Can't bridge CeDeFi networks. PURR can. The bar is now higher and so is the optionality.
items.tweetB: Every crypto treasury company before PURR diluted shareholders. PURR actively bought back shares at a discount. Now THYP exists as a baseline. The question for PURR leadership: can you consistently generate returns above spot HYPE? That's the only thing that justifies a premium. The ETF just made the benchmark explicit.

items.title: FX will signal the next bear market before equities — foreigners unhedged on dollar
items.idea: Foreigners are buying US mega-caps without hedging dollar risk. When DXY falls enough to drag on their returns, they sell US equities to repatriate — same mechanism as the 2025 tariff shock. The DXY and cross-currency basis swaps are the early warning signals, not the S&P 500 itself.
items.angle: Structural time bomb in positioning: larger unhedged foreign allocation to US equities = more violent unwind when dollar turns. Cross-currency basis moves weeks before equity selling materializes.
items.tweetA: The next US equity bear market will be signaled in FX before equities. Foreigners are buying US mega caps unhedged on dollar risk. When DXY falls enough to drag on their returns, they sell to repatriate — fast and correlated. Same mechanism as the 2025 tariff shock. Watch the cross-currency basis, not the VIX.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to this: the largest holders of US equities right now are foreigners who haven't hedged their dollar exposure. They bought US tech because AI capex is US-centric. They'll sell US tech when the dollar stops paying for that bet. The cross-currency basis swap is the canary. Most people aren't watching it.

charts.desc: Slide deck mentioned — Charts inside, worth opening

items.title: Impossible trinity by country: each forces pressure out of a different pipe
items.idea: Every country's impossible trinity forces macro stress through a different release valve. US: AI capex absorbs flows into mega-cap tech. Japan: FX is the release valve. Europe: equities absorb through underperformance. China: trade balance absorbs everything. This predicts in advance what asset class moves when stress builds.
items.angle: Map the pipe, predict the flow. Japan yen volatility is structurally mandated, not discretionary. European equity underperformance is structural, not cyclical. China trade surplus is an accounting identity.
items.tweetA: Best macro framework of the week: map each country's impossible trinity and you know where the pressure exits. US: into mega cap tech via AI flows. Japan: into yen via yield curve management. Europe: into equity underperformance. China: into trade balance. Understanding the pipe tells you what moves next.
items.tweetB: The question isn't 'will Japan intervene?' or 'why is European equities underperforming?' The answer is structural: impossible trinity. Japan's release valve is FX. Europe's is equities. You're not watching a random anomaly — you're watching accounting identities play out. Trade the pipe, not the narrative.

charts.desc: Slide deck — Charts inside, worth opening

items.title: Crude is the swing variable for next 2 weeks — breakdown triggers DAX/gold/silver short squeeze
items.idea: Crude is the single swing variable for the next two weeks. Crude breakdown: biggest short squeezes set up in DAX, Euro Stoxx, gold, silver, copper — all leveraged to energy import structure. Crude stays bid: range trading in equities with long end dragging on multiples. Either path structurally bullish; timing is the question.
items.angle: The asymmetry: long DAX hedge is long crude. A crude breakdown triggers violent reversal in energy-sensitive longs in Europe and commodities.
items.tweetA: Crude is the swing variable for the next 2 weeks. If it breaks down → short squeezes light up in DAX, Euro Stoxx, gold, silver, copper — all leveraged to energy import relief. If it stays bid → range trading with long end dragging on multiples. Either way, structurally bullish. The only question is timing. $DAX $GLD $SLV
items.tweetB: Here's a trade nobody's positioning for: a crude selloff is actually bullish DAX and European equities because those markets are short energy imports. The crowded European equity underperformance trade unwinds violently if oil breaks. The catalyst: any credible Iran headline. One phone call changes everything.

items.title: China April exports +14% YoY vs 8% estimate — technology is the primary driver
items.idea: China April exports grew 14% YoY, beating estimates of ~8%, despite elevated freight from the Iran war. Primary driver: technology exports. The export control regime is not slowing China's tech export engine — it's redirecting it to markets outside the US-China bilateral.
items.angle: More interesting than the number is where the exports are going — AI-adjacent hardware to the Global South the US isn't focused on. China is building hardware dependency elsewhere while the US debates bilateral chip policy.
items.tweetA: China April exports: +14% YoY vs 8% expected. Primary driver: technology. This is happening despite elevated freight from Hormuz. The US export control regime isn't slowing China's tech export engine — it's redirecting it. The customers aren't in the US. They're everywhere the US isn't looking.
items.tweetB: The China tech export surge (+14% vs 8% expected) is the number US policymakers should study more than any chip restriction. China is building hardware dependency across the Global South at scale. Export controls on advanced chips don't stop commodity-grade AI hardware from going everywhere else.

charts.desc: Chart showing China export growth surge; chart showing CSI 300 sector performance breakdown. Worth opening.

items.title: China 10yr below 2% while Japan yields significantly higher — decade-long dynamic inverted
items.idea: China's 10-year bond yields fell below 2%, while Japanese yields — long held at or below zero — are now significantly higher. These curves have crossed. The bond market is pricing structurally declined growth and inflation expectations in China ('Japanification' risk growing), while Japan normalizes upward.
items.angle: Most underappreciated macro chart of 2026. The China-Japan yield inversion signals that a decade of consensus about East Asian deflation/inflation dynamics is reversing simultaneously in both directions.
items.tweetA: China 10yr yields: below 2%. Japan 10yr yields: significantly above zero for the first time in years. These two curves have crossed. For a decade: Japan deflation/zero rates, China growth/higher rates. That world is ending. Both reversing simultaneously — in opposite directions.
items.tweetB: The China bond market is saying what official GDP data won't: growth and inflation expectations have structurally declined. Sub-2% 10yr yield is a statement. The Beijing stimulus narrative gets harder to believe against this signal. Meanwhile Japan is actually normalizing. The carry trade implications alone are enormous.

charts.desc: China vs Japan 10yr yield chart showing the crossover — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: US leads AI models, China leads AI deployment — 'best models won't matter if Americans won't use them'
items.idea: Abundance Institute's Neil Chilson argues the US and China are not running the same AI race. US leads on frontier model capability; China leads on deployment scale leveraging manufacturing and supply chain advantages. 'Having the best models in the world won't matter if the American people can't or won't use them.' The export control framework is built entirely on the model race assumption.
items.angle: If deployment is the variable that matters, restricting chip exports while failing to drive domestic AI adoption is a losing strategy. The US is winning the lab race and potentially losing the applied race.
items.tweetA: US vs China AI race: US leads on frontier models. China leads on deployment. 'Having the best models won't matter if the American people can't or won't use them.' — Neil Chilson, Abundance Institute. The export control framework is built entirely on the model race assumption. What if deployment is the variable that matters?
items.tweetB: The uncomfortable question from the Trump-Xi summit: what if China wins the AI race without ever matching GPT-5? Manufacturing scale + cheap inference + forced adoption = deployment wins at 90% of use cases. The US is optimizing for the 10% — frontier research — and calling it victory. That math deserves scrutiny.

charts.desc: Chart showing China's Made in China sector market share gains. Worth opening.

items.title: UK 30yr gilt yields hit 28-year high — Starmer fighting for survival, 4 ministers resigned
items.idea: UK 30-year gilt yields hit a 28-year high as four of Starmer's ministers resigned to force him to stand down. Authers draws the 1975 Britain parallel: stagflation, political paralysis, ailing PM. The bond market is pricing the absence of a credible fiscal anchor.
items.angle: Slower-motion than Liz Truss but structurally analogous. If gilt yields continue rising with political paralysis, it becomes a sovereign credibility event. Watch Labour MP defections in next 48 hours.
items.tweetA: UK 30yr gilts at a 28-year high. 4 of Starmer's ministers resigned to force him out. Bloomberg draws the 1975 Britain parallel — stagflation, ailing PM, political paralysis. The bond market is telling you there's no credible fiscal anchor. The Liz Truss episode was fast. This one is slow-motion.
items.tweetB: 30yr gilt yields at generational highs while the government is in political meltdown. This is what fiscal credibility risk looks like before it becomes a crisis. The UK is the live case study right now. Watch if Labour MPs break ranks in the next 48 hours — that's when the bond market accelerates.

items.title: CPI hot on headline, contained on core — marginal selloff is healthy weak-hand flush
items.idea: Today's CPI: hot on headline, contained on core. Inflation swaps rising, crude bid, bonds dragging on multiples slightly. Equities down only marginally after a 17% rally. Author's read: healthy washout of weak hands, not a bear signal. The structural thesis (rising inflation expectations + Fed pausing = real rates falling) is intact.
items.angle: The risk is if the pullback extends into the long end moving aggressively. Watch Warsh's first public communication — if he signals accommodation, the washout ends quickly; if he signals inflation concern, it extends.
items.tweetA: Hot CPI. Equities down marginally. After a 17% rally. Inflation swaps up, crude bid, bonds dragging slightly. This isn't a market in trouble — it's a weak-hand flush. The structural setup (rising inflation expectations + Fed pausing = real rates falling) hasn't changed. The timing has.
items.tweetB: The market reaction to today's hot CPI is telling: -0.16% on the S&P after a 17% rally. Either the market is completely wrong about the inflation threat, or it's correctly reading that the AI shock is strong enough to absorb higher rates at current levels. I'd rather bet on the second. Until Warsh signals otherwise.

items.title: China's 'Made in China' strategy: global market share gains in all 9 target sectors, partly at US expense
items.idea: Fathom Consulting's database tracking China's progress across 9 'Made in China' industrial strategy sectors shows substantial gains — Beijing increased global market share in each sector, partly at US expense. The Five-Year Plan advances self-sufficiency and high-tech manufacturing. Consumer recovery still not happening; tech and materials have rallied.
items.angle: The US is fighting a chip war. China is fighting a 9-sector industrial war simultaneously over decades. The chip restriction is a one-front battle in a nine-front war.
items.tweetA: Fathom Consulting tracks China's Made in China industrial strategy across 9 target sectors. Result: global market share gains in ALL 9, partly at US expense. The US is fighting a chip war. China is fighting a 9-sector industrial war. These are not the same conflict.
items.tweetB: China's Five-Year Plan 'lacks clearly measurable benchmarks' say analysts — and then they note it delivered market share gains in every single target sector from the previous plan. The absence of benchmarks is a feature, not a bug. It makes the strategy harder to attack and easier to adapt. Something Western policymakers might study.

charts.desc: Chart of China's Made in China sector progress; chart of CSI 300 sector breakdown. Worth opening.

items.title: Equal-weighted space stock basket up 17x from late-2022 lows — SpaceX IPO anticipation is a driver
items.idea: Equal-weighted basket of space stocks (RKLB, LUNR, RDW, VOYG, ASTS, PL, FLY, SPIR, BKSY, SATS) up 17x from late-2022 lows. Reflects commercial space becoming real plus SpaceX IPO anticipation. Still early: only proven launch services so far; orbital habitats, resource extraction, deep space are years away.
items.angle: SpaceX IPO is both the catalyst and the top risk. When the IPO date becomes concrete, that will be the critical inflection — watch for rotation from anticipation to realization.
items.tweetA: While everyone was watching AI stocks, an equal-weighted basket of space stocks (RKLB, LUNR, ASTS, BKSY et al.) quietly went up 17x from the late-2022 lows. Commercial space is going from dream to reality — and SpaceX IPO anticipation is adding fuel. We're still in the 'launch and satellites' phase. What comes after is much bigger.
items.tweetB: Space stocks 17x. SpaceX hasn't even IPO'd yet. The sector is at the stage where aviation was in the 1920s: a few proven operators, most companies burning cash, massive potential. The SpaceX IPO will either be the catalyst that brings institutional flows — or the moment everyone sells the news. Either way, it's the event.

charts.desc: Equal-weighted space stock basket chart from 2022 lows showing 17x return — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Space ETF market share at 0.04% vs 0.32% for A&D — 8x gap implies massive runway
items.idea: Space-focused ETFs (UFO, ARKX, NASA, ROKT, MARS) have 0.04% ETF market share vs 0.32% for Aerospace & Defense ETFs. US A&D is ~2% of total market cap. If space gets to A&D-level institutional ownership: 8x increase in ETF AUM flowing into sector. Average investor has essentially zero exposure.
items.angle: SpaceX IPO forces the classification decision for every index fund and thematic allocator. That's how 0.04% starts moving — not organic adoption but forced benchmark evaluation.
items.tweetA: Space ETFs (UFO, ARKX, NASA, ROKT, MARS) = 0.04% of ETF market share. Aerospace & Defense ETFs = 0.32%. A&D is ~2% of market cap. If space gets to A&D-level institutional ownership, that's 8x in category AUM. The average investor has zero exposure to a sector that just went 17x. That gap closes one way or another.
items.tweetB: 0.04% ETF market share in space. Two explanations: (1) smart money has looked and passed, or (2) smart money hasn't been forced to look yet. The SpaceX IPO forces the look. A $300B+ IPO with no pure-play ETF benchmark creates a classification problem for every index fund and thematic allocator. That's how 0.04% starts moving.

charts.desc: Space ETF vs A&D ETF market share comparison. Worth opening.
